questions.question: What does the SAMPLE history mnemonic stand for?
options: A. Signs/Symptoms, Allergies, Medications, Past history, Last intake, Events
B. Scene, Assessment, Monitoring, Priority, Location, Evaluation
C. Safety, Airway, Motion, Perfusion, Life threats, Exposure
D. Support, Attitude, Medication, Prognosis, Location, Escalation
questions.rationale: SAMPLE stands for Signs/Symptoms, Allergies, Medications, Past medical history, Last oral intake, and Events leading up to the incident.

questions.question: What pediatric-specific elements are added to the standard SAMPLE history?
options: A. Employment history
B. Birth history/developmental milestones, immunization status, and growth/weight trends
C. Credit history
D. Nothing is added for children
questions.rationale: Pediatric SAMPLE histories add emphasis on birth/developmental history for infants, immunization status, and growth/weight trends relevant to medication dosing.

questions.question: Who should be asked directly about their symptoms whenever possible?
options: A. Only caregivers, never the child
B. School-age children and adolescents, with caregiver accounts supplementing rather than replacing their report
C. Only infants
D. No one should be asked directly; observation alone suffices
questions.rationale: Older children and adolescents can usually provide reliable direct history, and their own report should be sought and supplemented (not replaced) by caregiver information.

questions.question: Which exam order is preferred for a frightened toddler?
options: A. Head-to-toe
B. Toe-to-head
C. Random order
D. Only auscultation, no other exam
questions.rationale: Starting with less threatening body areas (toe-to-head) improves cooperation in young, frightened children compared to the standard head-to-toe order.

questions.question: Which exam order is typically appropriate for a cooperative school-age child or adolescent?
options: A. Toe-to-head only
B. Standard head-to-toe sequence
C. No physical exam is needed
D. Only exam areas the child requests
questions.rationale: Older, more cooperative children can typically tolerate the standard head-to-toe exam sequence used in adult care.

questions.question: What is family-centered care?
options: A. Excluding caregivers from all pediatric encounters
B. Treating the caregiver as a partner in the child's care, including presence during many procedures
C. Allowing caregivers to make all treatment decisions without provider input
D. A billing category unrelated to clinical practice
questions.rationale: Family-centered care partners with caregivers rather than excluding them, recognizing their presence typically benefits the child without meaningfully slowing care.

questions.question: Does allowing a caregiver to remain present during care typically slow things down?
options: A. Yes, significantly
B. No — it usually reduces distress and improves cooperation without meaningfully slowing care
C. Only for infants
D. Only during transport, never during assessment
questions.rationale: Family-centered care research and practice support that caregiver presence generally improves the encounter without materially delaying care.

questions.question: A family does not speak English. What is the correct approach to communication?
options: A. Have the child interpret for their caregiver
B. Use professional interpretation services
C. Speak louder in English
D. Skip history-taking entirely
questions.rationale: Relying on a child to interpret medical information places an inappropriate burden on them and risks inaccurate communication; professional interpretation services are the correct approach.

questions.question: How should a provider approach a child with autism spectrum differences?
options: A. Use the identical approach for every patient regardless of the caregiver's input
B. Take cues from the caregiver about the child's usual communication style, minimize sensory overload, and allow extra time
C. Speak only to the caregiver and ignore the child entirely
D. Rush the exam to minimize contact time
questions.rationale: Individualized communication guided by the caregiver, reduced sensory overload, and allowing extra time improve the encounter for children with developmental differences.

questions.question: What is the difference between the focused exam and the rapid exam in secondary assessment?
options: A. They are identical
B. The focused exam targets the chief complaint for stable/low-priority patients; the rapid exam is more limited, for high-priority patients often performed en route
C. The rapid exam is only for infants
D. The focused exam is only used in the hospital
questions.rationale: The secondary assessment adapts to patient priority: a focused exam for stable patients on scene, versus a rapid, more limited exam for high-priority patients, often continued during transport.

questions.question: How should a provider communicate with a hearing-impaired caregiver?
options: A. Speak as loudly as possible from across the room
B. Face them directly, speak clearly, and use written notes or interpreter services as needed
C. Avoid direct communication entirely
D. Only communicate through the child
questions.rationale: Facing the person directly and speaking clearly (not necessarily loudly), supplemented by written notes or interpreter services, is the appropriate communication approach.

questions.question: What four steps make up PEPP's complete pediatric assessment sequence?
options: A. History, exam, transport, documentation
B. PAT, primary assessment, history, secondary assessment
C. Scene safety, vitals, treatment, discharge
D. Appearance, breathing, circulation, disability
questions.rationale: The full PEPP sequence is: Pediatric Assessment Triangle, primary assessment (ABCDE), history-taking, and secondary assessment — each building on the last.

questions.question: Why is growth/weight trend information part of a thorough pediatric SAMPLE history?
options: A. It is irrelevant to prehospital care
B. It informs weight-based medication dosing considerations
C. It is only used for insurance billing
D. It replaces the need for a chief complaint
questions.rationale: Weight/growth information is directly relevant to weight-based dosing considerations that may apply during subsequent care.

questions.question: Why is adapting communication for special needs considered a clinical skill rather than an optional courtesy?
options: A. It has no effect on care quality
B. It materially affects the quality and accuracy of the history and exam obtained
C. It is only relevant for documentation purposes
D. It is required only by hospital policy, not clinical necessity
questions.rationale: Communication adaptation directly impacts how much accurate, useful clinical information a provider can gather — making it a core clinical skill, not a courtesy.
